text.title: Distributed Pitfalls Catalog Core Rules
text.summary: Testable rules every application of Distributed Pitfalls Catalog must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every entry has: name, applies-when, evidence-source, deprecation date. Entries without applies-when are dropped — context is everything.

rationale.source: Pattern-catalog discipline (Gang of Four, 1994; Christopher Alexander, A Pattern

rule.rationale: Pattern-catalog discipline (Gang of Four, 1994; Christopher Alexander, A Pattern Language, 1977).

rule.statement: Entries are versioned. Removing an entry requires a tombstone with the date and reason so downstream callers can debug.
Catalog is curated by a named maintainer. PRs to add entries require the maintainer's review with a 'why this entry, not an existing one?' answer.

rationale.source: OSS curation lessons from Linux maintainer model

rule.rationale: OSS curation lessons from Linux maintainer model.

example.title: Anchored application
example.code: Trigger: Microservice extraction safety gate Artefact: structured output meeting the contract in content/02-output-contract.xml Evidence: ≥1 named source per rule (URL, ticket, transcript_id) Decision: downstream consumer can act without re-deriving reasoning
example.note: Demonstrates the closed loop the methodology creates — trigger → artefact → decision — with auditability at each step.

example.title: Un-anchored application
example.code: Trigger: 'we should do this thing' Artefact: prose paragraph, no schema Evidence: 'industry best practice' Decision: ambiguous — every reader interprets differently
example.note: Each ambiguity above is a failure mode catalogued in content/03-failure-modes.xml. The methodology exists to prevent exactly this shape.
